heroOverview: Pneumocystosis, caused by the atypical fungus Pneumocystis jirovecii (formerly P. carinii), remains the single most common serious opportunistic infection in people with advanced HIV disease worldwide, and accounts for approximately 30-40% of AIDS-related hospital admissions in settings where antiretroviral therapy is unavailable or has failed. The organism exclusively infects humans, cannot be cultured in the laboratory, and targets the alveoli of the lungs, producing a characteristic ground-glass pneumonia that can progress to respiratory failure within days. Diagnosis requires bronchoscopy or induced sputum with specific staining or PCR because standard cultures are negative. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (co-trimoxazole) cures the majority of cases and, at prophylactic doses, prevents recurrence — one of the most successful applications of antibiotic prophylaxis in modern medicine.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 300,000-400,000 HIV-positive people globally each year; prior to antiretroviral therapy, 80% of untreated AIDS patients developed at least one episode
keyStats.demographics: Primarily HIV-positive adults with CD4 counts below 200 cells/µL; also affects transplant recipients, patients on high-dose corticosteroids, and those receiving certain biological agents
keyStats.avgAge: Most HIV-associated cases in adults aged 25-50, reflecting HIV epidemic demographics
keyStats.globalCases: Remains among the most common AIDS-defining illnesses globally; incidence remains high in sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and parts of Latin America

definition: Pneumocystosis (ICD-10: B59) is pneumonia caused by Pneumocystis jirovecii, a microscopic atypical fungus in the phylum Ascomycota that is found widely in the environment but only causes clinical disease in hosts with severely impaired cellular immunity. Unlike most fungi, P. jirovecii has no ergosterol in its cell membrane (making it intrinsically resistant to azole and polyene antifungals), cannot be grown in standard laboratory culture, and appears to be transmitted by the airborne route between humans, with healthy seropositive adults serving as an asymptomatic reservoir. The organism attaches to alveolar epithelial cells, triggering an inflammatory response that fills the alveolar spaces with a characteristic foamy exudate visible on histology, impairing gas exchange and producing the clinical picture of progressive hypoxic pneumonia. The infection falls under the scope of infectious disease and pulmonary medicine. The risk threshold in HIV is a CD4 count below 200 cells/µL, and primary prophylaxis is recommended at that threshold; without prophylaxis, over 80% of people with AIDS develop PCP within their lifetime. In non-HIV immunocompromised patients, onset tends to be more acute and the clinical course more rapidly fatal.

typesClassification.description: The most common form, typically subacute over 2-6 weeks, with progressive exertional dyspnoea, dry cough, and low-grade fever; most patients present with CD4 counts below 100 cells/µL and bilateral ground-glass opacities on CT.

Affects transplant recipients, patients on high-dose corticosteroids or biological agents, and haematology patients; typically has a more abrupt onset, progresses faster, and has higher short-term mortality than HIV-associated PCP.

Rare and almost exclusively in advanced AIDS patients; T. marneffei can disseminate to the eyes, spleen, liver, lymph nodes, skin, and thyroid gland, presenting as unusual masses or lesions remote from the lungs.

primarySymptoms: Progressive shortness of breath on exertion, advancing over days to weeks until breathlessness occurs at rest — the cardinal symptom of PCP and often the first sign the patient notices.
A dry, non-productive cough without significant sputum production, reflecting alveolar inflammation rather than airway infection.
Low-grade fever, typically 37.5-38.5°C, persisting for weeks and accompanying the respiratory deterioration.
Hypoxia — oxygen saturation dropping below 94% on room air, often with a disproportionate desaturation on minimal exertion, a hallmark finding in PCP.
Chest tightness or a sense of chest constriction, reported by many patients as more prominent than frank pain.
Fatigue and lethargy that worsen in parallel with the degree of hypoxia and the body's inflammatory response.
Rapid respiratory rate (tachypnoea) with shallow breathing, reflecting the effort to maintain oxygenation through impaired lungs.
Night sweats, particularly in the HIV-associated form, overlapping with the constitutional symptoms of advanced HIV itself.
Weight loss over the weeks preceding diagnosis, from the combined effect of the infection and underlying immunosuppression.
Cyanosis — a bluish discolouration of the lips or fingertips — in advanced cases with severe hypoxia.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained breathlessness with climbing stairs or light activity in an HIV-positive patient with CD4 below 200 cells/µL.
A new dry cough lasting more than a week with no clear respiratory tract infection features.
Oxygen saturation that drops more than 3-5% with minimal exercise ('exercise desaturation') while resting saturation remains near normal.
Low-grade fever with progressive fatigue and no localising findings in an immunocompromised patient.
emergencySigns: Oxygen saturation below 90% on room air at rest, indicating severe gas exchange failure requiring hospital admission.
Rapid breathing above 30 breaths per minute at rest with accessory muscle use, signalling imminent respiratory failure.
Bilateral extensive ground-glass opacities on chest CT with severe hypoxia in a patient already on treatment — an indication for ICU assessment.
Pneumothorax complicating PCP, presenting as sudden worsening of dyspnoea with pleuritic chest pain — a known, life-threatening complication.
Haemodynamic instability or altered consciousness accompanying respiratory distress — signs of multi-organ failure.

causes.cause: Depletion of CD4 T-lymphocytes below 200 cells/µL
causes.description: CD4 T-helper cells coordinate both macrophage activation to clear P. jirovecii and antibody production against the organism; once the CD4 count falls below 200 cells/µL in HIV infection, this defence fails and the organism proliferates unchecked in alveoli, triggering the inflammatory response that impairs gas exchange.

causes.cause: Airborne acquisition from the environment or infected individuals
causes.description: Epidemiological and molecular typing studies show that P. jirovecii is transmitted through the air, likely from asymptomatic carriers as well as infected patients; colonisation rates in healthy adults with normal immunity reach 20-65% in cross-sectional serological surveys, with overt pneumonia only emerging when immunity collapses.

causes.cause: Reactivation of latent P. jirovecii colonisation
causes.description: Serological data suggest that many adults are exposed to P. jirovecii in early childhood and carry it asymptomatically; PCP in immunocompromised adults may represent reactivation of latent organisms rather than new acquisition, explaining why spatial clustering of cases is not always observed.

Steroids suppress T-cell function and macrophage activation; doses equivalent to prednisone above 20 mg/day for more than 4 weeks create substantial PCP risk in non-HIV patients, and prophylaxis is recommended during steroid courses at this level in practice guidelines.

Cyclosporin and tacrolimus specifically target T-cell activation pathways; transplant recipients are at peak risk for PCP in the first year post-transplant when immunosuppression is heaviest, accounting for approximately 5-15% of pneumocystosis cases outside HIV.

riskFactors.factor: HIV/AIDS with CD4 count below 200 cells/µL
riskFactors.description: The dominant risk factor for over 90% of cases globally; the risk becomes statistically significant below 200 cells/µL and accelerates sharply below 100 cells/µL, where most actual episodes occur.

riskFactors.factor: Failure to take PCP prophylaxis when indicated
riskFactors.description: Non-adherence to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis in eligible HIV patients is directly responsible for the majority of preventable PCP episodes; prophylaxis reduces incidence by approximately 80-90%.

riskFactors.factor: Solid organ transplantation in the first 12 months
riskFactors.description: Transplant recipients, particularly lung and heart-lung transplant patients, face the highest post-transplant PCP risk; prophylaxis for 6-12 months post-transplant reduces this risk dramatically and is standard of care.

Induction chemotherapy for leukaemia and lymphoma, particularly regimens including high-dose steroids, depletes lymphocytes and creates a window of PCP susceptibility during which prophylaxis is recommended.

Agents such as rituximab, alemtuzumab, fludarabine, and TNF inhibitors deplete lymphocytes or impair T-cell function; PCP prophylaxis guidelines for these agents exist in rheumatology, oncology, and transplant medicine.
Conditions such as severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) and DiGeorge syndrome cause profound T-cell defects that make PCP a frequent presenting infection, often in infancy or childhood.
Malnutrition impairs cellular immunity and has been associated with pneumocystosis in malnourished children without recognised HIV infection, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa.

affectedDemographics: HIV-positive adults with CD4 counts below 200 cells/µL not on antiretroviral therapy or prophylaxis
Lung and heart transplant recipients in the first 12 months post-transplant
Patients receiving intensive chemotherapy for leukaemia, lymphoma, or other haematological cancers
Patients on long-term high-dose corticosteroids (equivalent to prednisone >20 mg/day for >4 weeks)
Infants with severe combined immunodeficiency or other primary T-cell defects
Patients receiving rituximab, alemtuzumab, or other lymphocyte-depleting agents
diagnosisOverview: Pneumocystosis is diagnosed by demonstrating P. jirovecii in respiratory specimens because the organism cannot be cultured. The diagnostic approach follows a sequence determined by clinical urgency and available resources. Chest radiography in PCP classically shows bilateral perihilar interstitial infiltrates, though it may be normal early in the course. High-resolution CT of the chest is more sensitive and shows the highly characteristic bilateral ground-glass opacities in a geographic distribution, sometimes with cysts or pneumatoceles. When imaging and clinical presentation are consistent, definitive microbiological confirmation is obtained from induced sputum (less invasive, sensitivity 50-90%) or bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) via flexible bronchoscopy (sensitivity 85-98%, the gold standard). Specimens are stained with Giemsa, Gomori methenamine silver (GMS), or immunofluorescence techniques to demonstrate the characteristic cysts and trophic forms. PCR-based detection has largely supplanted conventional staining at major centres, offering higher sensitivity on induced sputum and allowing quantification to distinguish colonisation from infection. Serum beta-D-glucan is elevated in over 90% of PCP cases and is a useful screening marker; a normal value in an immunocompromised patient with respiratory symptoms argues strongly against the diagnosis. LDH (lactate dehydrogenase) is elevated in most PCP cases and correlates with severity. The alveolar-arterial oxygen gradient is used for severity stratification and guides steroid use. Blood gases or pulse oximetry both at rest and with exercise are essential at presentation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the bilateral ground-glass opacities characteristic of PCP; normal HRCT virtually excludes active pneumocystosis in a symptomatic immunocompromised patient.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in a CT scanner for about 10 minutes; no contrast injection is usually needed for chest CT in this context.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold-standard diagnostic procedure with sensitivity of 85-98% for P. jirovecii; lavage fluid is stained or tested by PCR for definitive microbiological confirmation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible camera tube is passed through your nose or mouth into the lung airways under light sedation; sterile saline is flushed in and retrieved; the procedure takes 20-40 minutes and you need monitoring for 2-4 hours afterwards.

diagnosticTests.test: Induced sputum with Giemsa/GMS stain or PCR
diagnosticTests.purpose: A less invasive alternative to bronchoscopy; sensitivity is 50-90% with experienced technique; PCR is more sensitive than conventional staining.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A respiratory therapist guides you to breathe nebulised hypertonic saline to provoke a deep cough that brings up sputum from the lower airways; the procedure takes 15-30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A broad fungal marker elevated in more than 90% of PCP cases; a high value strongly supports the diagnosis and may justify empirical treatment while awaiting bronchoscopy results.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results available within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the degree of hypoxia and alveolar-arterial oxygen gradient, which determines disease severity and whether adjunctive corticosteroids are indicated.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample is taken from the radial artery at the wrist under local anaesthesia; there is a sharp but brief sting; results are available within minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated in most PCP cases due to alveolar cell damage; serial LDH measurements can monitor treatment response — a falling LDH supports improvement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Part of a standard blood panel; a single venous blood draw is all that is required.

diagnosticTests.test: CD4 count and HIV viral load
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the degree of immune suppression, guides the indication for primary prophylaxis, and informs the decision to start or intensify antiretroviral therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venous blood draw; results typically available within 24-48 hours.

treatmentOverview: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX, co-trimoxazole) at high dose is the first-line treatment for all severity levels of PCP and the most effective agent available. For mild-to-moderate disease, oral TMP-SMX (15-20 mg/kg/day of the trimethoprim component, divided into 3-4 doses) for 21 days achieves clinical cure in approximately 85-90% of patients. Intravenous TMP-SMX is used for moderate-to-severe disease or when oral absorption is doubtful. In patients with moderate-to-severe PCP, defined by a partial pressure of oxygen below 70 mmHg or alveolar-arterial oxygen gradient above 35 mmHg, adjunctive corticosteroids (prednisolone 40 mg twice daily for 5 days, then tapered over 21 days) have been shown in randomised trials to reduce mortality by approximately 50% by limiting the hyperinflammatory response in the alveoli. Pentamidine (intravenous 4 mg/kg/day) is the main alternative for TMP-SMX intolerance, but carries significant side effects including pancreatitis, nephrotoxicity, and hypoglycaemia. Atovaquone suspension is used for mild-to-moderate disease in TMP-SMX-intolerant patients but requires food for absorption and is less effective in severe disease. Clindamycin plus primaquine is another salvage option. In HIV-associated PCP, antiretroviral therapy should be started within 2 weeks of PCP diagnosis rather than waiting for treatment completion; this timing, established in clinical trials, reduces AIDS mortality without increasing IRIS risk substantially. After completing 21 days of treatment, secondary prophylaxis with TMP-SMX double-strength three times weekly or daily is continued until CD4 count exceeds 200 cells/µL on ART. Supplemental oxygen and, in severe cases, mechanical ventilation are essential supportive measures.

medicalTreatments.name: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) — oral
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for all PCP severity levels; given at 15-20 mg/kg/day of the TMP component divided three to four times daily for 21 days. Also available as an intravenous formulation for moderate-to-severe disease. Adverse effects include rash, nausea, neutropenia, and elevated creatinine; these occur in approximately 25-50% of HIV patients but are less frequent in non-HIV patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical success rates of approximately 85-90% in HIV-associated PCP when the full 21-day course is completed.

medicalTreatments.name: Adjunctive corticosteroids (prednisolone or methylprednisolone)
medicalTreatments.description: Recommended for moderate-to-severe PCP (PaO2 below 70 mmHg); prednisolone 40 mg twice daily for 5 days, then 40 mg once daily for 5 days, then 20 mg once daily for 11 days. Must be started within 72 hours of antifungal therapy initiation to achieve maximal benefit.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality in moderate-to-severe PCP by approximately 50% in randomised controlled trials — one of the most significant advances in PCP management in the 1990s.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous pentamidine
medicalTreatments.description: Given at 4 mg/kg/day IV as a second-line agent for TMP-SMX-intolerant or failing patients; carries substantial toxicity including nephrotoxicity, hypoglycaemia, pancreatitis, and hypotension, requiring close monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response in approximately 70-80% of TMP-SMX-intolerant patients; inferior to TMP-SMX in head-to-head studies but an important salvage option.

medicalTreatments.name: Atovaquone suspension
medicalTreatments.description: Oral alternative for mild-to-moderate PCP in patients who cannot tolerate TMP-SMX; must be taken with fatty food to achieve adequate absorption. Less effective than TMP-SMX in severe disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical success rate of approximately 70-75% in mild-to-moderate PCP, inferior to TMP-SMX (85-90%) but with significantly better tolerability.

medicalTreatments.name: Clindamycin plus primaquine (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: A salvage combination for TMP-SMX-intolerant patients; clindamycin 300-450 mg every 6 hours plus primaquine 15-30 mg base daily for 21 days; contraindicated in G6PD deficiency due to primaquine-induced haemolysis risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates of approximately 80-85% comparable to TMP-SMX in mild-to-moderate disease; limited data in severe cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Chest tube drainage for pneumothorax
surgicalOptions.description: PCP-related pneumatocele rupture can cause pneumothorax; a chest tube is inserted to re-expand the collapsed lung. This is a complication-management procedure, not part of primary PCP treatment.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Re-expansion of the pneumothorax is achieved in approximately 80-90% of cases; however, bronchopleural fistula can complicate PCP-related pneumothorax and may require surgical intervention.

alternativeTreatments.name: Antiretroviral therapy initiation
alternativeTreatments.description: Starting ART within 2 weeks of PCP diagnosis is the most important co-intervention in HIV-associated PCP; restoring CD4 immunity prevents relapse and all future opportunistic infections.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional and respiratory support
alternativeTreatments.description: Adequate nutrition, maintained oxygenation via nasal cannula or mask, and avoiding unnecessary intubation (non-invasive ventilation where possible) improve outcomes during the acute phase.

whySeeSpecialist: Any immunocompromised patient with new breathlessness and bilateral ground-glass opacities on CT should be assessed by an infectious disease physician alongside a pulmonologist. Bronchoscopy for BAL, the definitive diagnostic procedure, is performed by a pulmonologist or interventional physician. HIV specialists should be involved early to optimise antiretroviral therapy timing. In mechanically ventilated patients, intensivists are essential.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an infectious disease specialist at an HIV referral centre or teaching hospital with a pulmonology team experienced in bronchoscopy for immunocompromised patients. Centres that manage HIV opportunistic infections routinely have the most experience with PCP treatment protocols and co-trimoxazole dosing, drug interactions, and the timing of ART initiation during active PCP.
preventionStrategies: Start TMP-SMX prophylaxis (one double-strength tablet three times weekly or daily) in all HIV-positive patients when CD4 count falls below 200 cells/µL.
Continue PCP prophylaxis post-transplant for at least 6-12 months; lung transplant recipients may need lifelong prophylaxis.
Maintain PCP prophylaxis in haematology patients receiving induction chemotherapy with high-dose steroids or lymphocyte-depleting agents.
Discontinue prophylaxis only after CD4 count has been sustained above 200 cells/µL for at least 3 months on effective ART, per DHHS and WHO guidelines.
Dapsone or atovaquone are alternative prophylactic agents for patients who cannot tolerate TMP-SMX.
Screen and manage HIV proactively so that CD4 counts never fall to PCP risk threshold in patients with access to ART.
lifestyleModifications: Take prophylactic TMP-SMX on schedule; never stop without discussing with your HIV or transplant physician.
Attend regular CD4 count monitoring to know when prophylaxis can be safely stopped.
Avoid smoking, which compounds the respiratory damage from PCP and worsens long-term lung function.
Report new breathlessness or a dry cough lasting more than a few days immediately rather than waiting; early treatment dramatically reduces severity.
Practice respiratory hygiene when around other immunocompromised people, as person-to-person airborne transmission of P. jirovecii has been documented in hospital settings.

recommended: High-protein diet to maintain muscle mass and support immune recovery during prolonged illness
Adequate caloric intake to prevent malnutrition, which impairs treatment tolerability and recovery
Folate-rich foods (leafy greens, legumes) during TMP-SMX therapy, as the drug can inhibit folate metabolism
Fatty food or a full meal with each dose of atovaquone suspension to maximise drug absorption
avoid: Alcohol during treatment, as TMP-SMX and pentamidine interact adversely with hepatic alcohol metabolism
Sulphite-rich foods if there is a sulphonamide sensitivity history

exerciseGuidelines: During acute PCP, physical exertion must be strictly limited — even walking short distances can cause dangerous oxygen desaturation. Bed rest with supplemental oxygen is the standard approach during hospitalisation. After clinical improvement and discharge, gentle graduated activity is reintroduced under guidance, with pulse oximetry monitoring if there is residual lung impairment. Formal pulmonary rehabilitation may be appropriate after severe PCP with lasting lung function deficit.
dailyManagement: Complete the full 21-day course of TMP-SMX; stopping early risks relapse and development of resistance.
Take the prophylaxis dose exactly as prescribed after treatment is complete; this prevents a second episode, which has even higher mortality.
Use a pulse oximeter at home if your doctor recommends it to detect oxygen desaturation early during recovery.
Report any worsening breathlessness, new chest pain, or sudden sharp worsening, which may indicate pneumothorax.
Start antiretroviral therapy within 2 weeks of diagnosis if you have HIV and are not already on treatment.
prognosis: The prognosis for PCP has improved dramatically since the introduction of prophylaxis and effective antiretroviral therapy. For HIV-associated PCP treated with standard co-trimoxazole, overall in-hospital mortality is approximately 10-15% for mild-to-moderate disease. Severe PCP requiring mechanical ventilation carries mortality of 50-60% even with optimal treatment. Adjunctive corticosteroids reduce mortality in moderate-to-severe disease by approximately 50%, and their routine use has substantially improved outcomes since the late 1980s. Non-HIV patients with PCP — transplant recipients and those on biological agents — have higher short-term mortality than HIV patients, approaching 30-50% in some series, because their illness tends to present more acutely and progress faster. Long-term, patients who achieve immune reconstitution on ART rarely develop a second episode of PCP. Those who recover from severe PCP may retain residual lung function impairment including reduced diffusion capacity and exercise intolerance for months to years.
recoveryTimeline: Clinical improvement with TMP-SMX typically begins within 4-8 days of treatment initiation. Worsening in the first 3-5 days of therapy despite microbiological sensitivity is common and is attributed to the inflammatory response to dying organisms; this is the rationale for adjunctive corticosteroids. Radiological clearing of ground-glass opacities lags behind clinical improvement by 2-6 weeks. Full symptomatic recovery after mild-to-moderate PCP takes 4-8 weeks; after severe PCP requiring ventilation, recovery may take months, and some patients have lasting exercise limitation. Secondary prophylaxis continues for months and is discontinued only after sustained immune recovery.
complications: Respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation — occurs in approximately 15-30% of severe PCP cases; carrying mortality of 50-60%.
Pneumothorax — complicates 2-5% of PCP cases due to rupture of subpleural cysts (pneumatoceles); may be bilateral and tension pneumothorax is a medical emergency.

Paradoxical worsening (IRIS) after ART initiation — worsening oxygenation and radiological changes 2-8 weeks after starting antiretroviral therapy, requiring careful management to distinguish treatment failure from immune reconstitution.
TMP-SMX drug toxicity — rash, neutropenia, elevated creatinine, and hepatotoxicity occur in 25-50% of HIV patients on treatment-dose TMP-SMX; dose adjustment or drug switch may be required.
Pneumatocele formation — thin-walled lung cysts developing during PCP that persist after treatment and predispose to pneumothorax; often resolve over months.

supportResources.description: U.S. DHHS guidelines on prevention and treatment of Pneumocystis pneumonia in adults and adolescents with HIV.

supportResources.name: NHS — Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP)
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK patient guide to PCP symptoms, treatment, and prevention.

supportResources.name: CDC — Pneumocystis Pneumonia
supportResources.description: CDC overview of PCP, risk populations, and prevention strategies.

sources.title: NIH DHHS — Guidelines for the Prevention and Treatment of Opportunistic Infections in Adults and Adolescents with HIV: Pneumocystis Pneumonia
Bozzette SA et al. A Controlled Trial of Early Adjunctive Treatment with Corticosteroids for Pneumocystis carinii Pneumonia in AIDS. New England Journal of Medicine, 1990
Huang L et al. HIV-Associated Pneumocystis Pneumonia. Proceedings of the American Thoracic Society, 2011

confusedWithConditions.name: Pulmonary tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause bilateral pulmonary infiltrates and constitutional symptoms in HIV patients, but TB is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, typically shows upper-lobe or cavitary changes rather than diffuse ground-glass opacities, and is confirmed by AFB smear or GeneXpert.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cytomegaloviral pneumonitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CMV pneumonitis causes bilateral interstitial infiltrates in immunocompromised patients but is confirmed by detection of CMV in BAL or tissue, and does not respond to TMP-SMX; the beta-D-glucan is negative in CMV pneumonitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Aspergillosis primarily affects neutropenic patients rather than CD4-depleted HIV patients, produces nodules and halo signs on CT rather than diffuse ground-glass opacities, and is diagnosed by serum galactomannan and culture rather than beta-D-glucan and BAL staining.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bacterial pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bacterial pneumonia typically has a more acute onset with productive purulent cough, focal consolidation on chest imaging, and responds to antibiotics; PCP is subacute with a dry cough, bilateral ground-glass pattern, negative bacterial cultures, and elevated beta-D-glucan.

relatedConditions.name: Pulmonary cryptococcosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are AIDS-defining opportunistic fungal infections targeting the lungs; the two conditions must be differentiated because treatment is entirely different, with cryptococcosis requiring amphotericin B plus flucytosine rather than TMP-SMX.

relatedConditions.name: Pulmonary toxoplasmosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Pulmonary toxoplasmosis is a rare HIV opportunistic infection that can mimic PCP with bilateral infiltrates and hypoxia; it is managed with pyrimethamine-sulfadiazine rather than TMP-SMX monotherapy.

relatedConditions.name: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are life-threatening pulmonary fungal infections in immunocompromised patients; they may co-exist, and their radiological and clinical distinction informs antifungal selection.

relatedConditions.name: Pulmonary candidiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Pulmonary candidiasis, though rare as primary pneumonia, is a diagnostic consideration in the same immunocompromised host population, distinguishable by culture and the absence of ground-glass opacities on CT.

faqs.question: What is PCP (Pneumocystis pneumonia)?
faqs.answer: PCP is a serious lung infection caused by a fungus called Pneumocystis jirovecii. It mainly affects people with weakened immune systems, especially those with HIV/AIDS who have CD4 counts below 200. It causes progressive breathlessness, dry cough, fever, and low oxygen levels, and can be life-threatening without treatment.

faqs.question: What causes PCP in HIV?
faqs.answer: PCP in HIV occurs when the CD4 T-cell count falls below 200 cells per microlitre, impairing the cellular immunity needed to control Pneumocystis jirovecii. The organism, which colonises healthy adults without causing harm, multiplies in the alveoli when immune defences fail, filling the air sacs with inflammatory exudate that blocks oxygen transfer.

faqs.question: How is PCP treated?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX, co-trimoxazole) at high dose for 21 days. Moderate-to-severe cases also receive adjunctive corticosteroids, which reduce mortality by approximately 50%. For patients who cannot tolerate TMP-SMX, alternatives include pentamidine, atovaquone, or clindamycin-primaquine.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of Pneumocystis pneumonia?
faqs.answer: The hallmark symptoms are progressive shortness of breath on exertion, a dry non-productive cough, and low-grade fever developing over days to weeks. Oxygen levels may drop significantly even with mild activity. Fatigue, night sweats, and chest tightness are also common. The onset is typically gradual compared to bacterial pneumonia.

faqs.question: How is PCP diagnosed?
faqs.answer: PCP is diagnosed by bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL), which samples lung fluid for microscopy or PCR testing for Pneumocystis jirovecii. Induced sputum is a less invasive first step with 50-90% sensitivity. Serum beta-D-glucan is elevated in over 90% of cases and supports early diagnosis.

faqs.question: Can PCP be prevented?
faqs.answer: Yes. TMP-SMX prophylaxis (one double-strength tablet three times weekly or daily) reduces PCP incidence by approximately 80-90% in HIV patients with CD4 below 200 cells/µL. Transplant recipients and patients on immunosuppressive therapy also receive prophylaxis. Prevention also depends on maintaining CD4 counts through antiretroviral therapy.

faqs.question: Is PCP contagious?
faqs.answer: Pneumocystis jirovecii is transmitted through the air, and immunocompromised patients can acquire it from healthy carriers or from other infected patients in healthcare settings. However, healthy people clear the organism without developing disease. Isolation precautions are sometimes recommended in wards with multiple immunocompromised patients.

faqs.question: Why is PCP associated with HIV?
faqs.answer: Pneumocystis jirovecii requires intact CD4 T-cell immunity to be contained. HIV progressively destroys CD4 cells, and when the count falls below 200 cells per microlitre, the immune defence that normally suppresses Pneumocystis colonisation breaks down, allowing the organism to multiply in the lungs and cause pneumonia.

faqs.question: What does PCP look like on a chest CT?
faqs.answer: CT chest in PCP characteristically shows bilateral, symmetrical ground-glass opacities — a hazy whitening of the lung fields — with a geographic distribution sparing the periphery early in the course. Thin-walled cysts (pneumatoceles) may be visible. This pattern differs from the lobar consolidation of bacterial pneumonia.

faqs.question: What is the mortality rate of PCP?
faqs.answer: With standard TMP-SMX treatment, in-hospital mortality for HIV-associated PCP is approximately 10-15% for mild-to-moderate disease. Severe PCP requiring mechanical ventilation has mortality of 50-60%. Non-HIV patients (transplant recipients, cancer patients) have higher mortality of 30-50% because the illness progresses more rapidly.

faqs.question: When should I start ART if I have PCP?
faqs.answer: Antiretroviral therapy should be started within 2 weeks of beginning PCP treatment in HIV patients not already on ART. This timing has been shown in clinical trials to reduce AIDS mortality compared to deferring ART until after PCP treatment is complete, without substantially increasing the risk of immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome.

faqs.question: What is beta-D-glucan and why is it used in PCP?
faqs.answer: Beta-D-glucan is a component of the cell wall of most fungi, including Pneumocystis jirovecii. Serum beta-D-glucan is elevated in over 90% of PCP cases and is used as a non-specific screening marker. A normal beta-D-glucan level in an immunocompromised patient with respiratory symptoms makes PCP less likely and may avoid the need for bronchoscopy.

faqs.question: Can PCP cause a pneumothorax?
faqs.answer: Yes. PCP can cause thin-walled air-filled cysts (pneumatoceles) in the lungs that may rupture and collapse the lung (pneumothorax). This complication occurs in 2-5% of PCP cases and is suggested by sudden sharp worsening of breathlessness. Tension pneumothorax is a medical emergency requiring immediate chest tube insertion.

faqs.question: Is PCP a bacterial or fungal infection?
faqs.answer: PCP is a fungal infection, caused by Pneumocystis jirovecii, which is classified as an atypical fungus in the phylum Ascomycota. Despite the word 'pneumonia' and its historical classification as a protozoan, it does not respond to standard antibiotics. It has a unique cell membrane without ergosterol, making azole and polyene antifungals ineffective.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from PCP?
faqs.answer: Symptomatic improvement typically begins within 4-8 days of TMP-SMX therapy. Radiological clearing of ground-glass opacities takes 2-6 weeks. Full symptomatic recovery after mild disease takes 4-8 weeks. After severe PCP requiring ventilation, recovery may take several months, and some patients retain lasting exercise limitations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PCP prophylaxis and treatment doses?
faqs.answer: Treatment-dose TMP-SMX is 15-20 mg/kg/day of trimethoprim divided three to four times daily for 21 days — a high dose targeting active infection. Prophylaxis uses one double-strength tablet (160 mg trimethoprim / 800 mg sulfamethoxazole) three times weekly or daily — a much lower dose that prevents the organism from establishing infection in at-risk patients.

faqs.question: Can PCP affect organs other than the lungs?
faqs.answer: Extrapulmonary pneumocystosis is rare and occurs almost exclusively in people with advanced HIV. The organism can disseminate to the spleen, liver, lymph nodes, eyes, skin, thyroid, and adrenal glands. Before routine PCP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX, extrapulmonary disease was more commonly reported; it is now uncommon in settings with access to prophylaxis.

faqs.question: Does PCP affect children?
faqs.answer: PCP can affect infants with HIV (often perinatally acquired), those with severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID), and children undergoing intensive chemotherapy. In HIV-negative infants with SCID, PCP may be the presenting illness of the immune defect. Presentation and treatment principles are similar to adults, but weight-based dosing and age-specific considerations apply.

faqs.question: What should I do if I am on immunosuppressants and develop breathlessness?
faqs.answer: Seek medical review promptly. Any new progressive breathlessness, dry cough, or reduced oxygen levels in an immunosuppressed person should be investigated for PCP and other opportunistic pulmonary infections. Do not assume it is a minor respiratory illness. Early CT chest and beta-D-glucan testing can speed diagnosis and allow treatment to start before severe hypoxia develops.

faqs.question: Can PCP come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Without secondary prophylaxis, PCP recurs in approximately 30-50% of HIV patients within 6-12 months. Starting or continuing TMP-SMX prophylaxis after treatment reduces this risk by over 80%. Prophylaxis can be safely discontinued once CD4 count is sustained above 200 cells/µL for at least 3 months on antiretroviral therapy.

metaDescription: Pneumocystosis (PCP) is a life-threatening fungal pneumonia causing dyspnoea and hypoxia in immunocompromised patients. Learn symptoms, diagnosis and treatment.
